heading-reasons: 15 Reasons People Might See a Sexological Bodyworker

list-reasons.accepting: Accepting and loving one’s body, gender and/or sexuality
list-reasons.pleasure: Learning how to ask for and receive pleasure
list-reasons.habits-patterns: Becoming aware of habits and patterns, and learning to expand pleasure throughout the body
list-reasons.boundaries: Learning about boundaries and consensual touch
list-reasons.healthy-porn: Developing a healthy use of porn
list-reasons.pain-relief: Relief from pelvic pain or numbness
list-reasons.enhance: Couples/Triads/Quads wanting to enhance their sex life
list-reasons.referred-by: Referred by a Therapist, Doctor, Sexologist, Massage Therapist

check-in.label: Check-in
check-in.desc: You and Gerard discuss your intentions, interests, challenges and sensual/sexual history.

body-scan.label: Body Scan
body-scan.desc: Gerard leads you in a somatic body scan to bring awareness to sensations in your body. This practice helps with learning to make choices from an embodied place.

options.label: Options
options.desc: Based on the check-in and body scan Gerard will offer you 2 options for bodywork or somatic practices that could be useful based on your specific interests or challenges.

agreement.label: Agreement
agreement.desc: You choose 1 somatic practice or type of bodywork. You and Gerard agree on the boundaries.

integration.label: Integration
integration.desc: The session ends with a few minutes of integration to ‘save’ the learning experience in your nervous system (mind and body), thus forming new healthy habits and expanding your potential for pleasure.

paragraph-society-1: We all set limits on the quantity and quality of pleasure we can embody. Limits to pleasure are ubiquitous in a culture that shames sexuality and fails to teach us how to honour and celebrate our erotic selves. Sexual wounding becomes embedded in the body’s tissues and responses. Somatic Sex Education and Bodywork can assist clients in expanding the pleasure that is possible. This expansion has physical, mental, emotional, and relational benefits.
paragraph-society-2: In 2019, I graduated from the esteemed Institute of Somatic Sexology in Australia to become a Certified Sexological Bodyworker. I warmly welcome people of all genders, races, cultures, religions, sexual orientations and relationship choices to work with me.
paragraph-somatic-1: Sexological Bodyworkers teach embodied sex education and offer bodywork. We teach through body experiences designed to nurture, deepen or awaken the sensual self. Our teaching can include coaching in breathwork, body awareness, movement, boundary-setting (consent), communication, anatomy, massage, mindful masturbation, scar tissue remediation and other body-based practices.
paragraph-somatic-2: Not all teachings include touch, but when requested touch is one-way (from practitioner to client) and is client-led. We touch in ways that assist clients in developing presence within the body, opening interior awareness, empowering choice and voice, and learning how the body can feel safer and more alive. Uniquely in the profession, we are trained to offer client-led genital or anal touch for the purposes of education, healing and pleasure. Sexological Bodyworkers are trauma-informed and remain clothed during sessions.
paragraph-about-intro: I'm a Certified Sexological Bodyworker (Australian Institute of Somatic Sexology, 2019) with 25 years of training in dance, somatics, yoga, and meditation. After studying with master teachers Deej Juventin, Uma Ayelet, Joseph Kramer and Ellen Heed, in 2020 I deepened my training through mentorship with Ron Stuart and Pono Stuart, two Certified Sexological Bodyworkers associated with the Institute for the Study of Somatic Sex Education (Victoria, Canada). I maintain dialogue and ongoing supervision by practicing with colleagues, and continue to learn and integrate practices from dance, yoga, and massage traditions into my work.
paragraph-about-1: I came to Somatic Sexology through my artistic project, Public / Private Parts, which began with the question, ‘Why are so-called liberated societies still afraid of sex?’ Through research interviews with sex workers I discovered the modality of Sexological Bodywork and shortly thereafter completed my 6-month embodied training. Sexological Bodywork helped me to see human sexuality from a variety of perspectives : culturally, sociologically, anatomically, neuro-scientifically and relationally.
paragraph-about-2: As a non-binary and queer person (they/he), it has taken me decades of work to feel comfortable in my skin. My 25 years of training in dance, somatics, yoga and meditation helped me discover a lot of pleasure in my body and with others. But it wasn’t until my Sexological Bodywork training that I learned to integrate my genitals into the rest of my body; this gave me the tools to truly listen to all of it. One of the major lessons was the Wheel of Consent, which gave me a framework to understand relationship dynamics, power and choice. This embodied lesson has improved both my sexual life and my overall life – because I find the lessons I learn in sex often also apply to my friends and my work too.
paragraph-about-3: I am deeply passionate about sharing this modality with others to expand intimacy, develop presence, sensitivity, and connection between body, mind and spirit. I welcome all people to this work – regardless of gender, culture, race, religion, sexual orientation, and ability, or whereever you’re starting.
paragraph-pricing-1: * Prices are in Canadian dollars and include taxes
paragraph-pricing-2: I can accommodate couples/triads, shorter sessions, financial difficulty, etc. Write to tell me about your situation!
paragraph-covid: NOTE: For in-person sessions, I follow public health guidelines to reduce the transmission of COVID-19 and ask that all clients abide by these guidelines, which are subject to change.
paragraph-thanks: Thank you to Sexological Bodyworker Caffyn Jesse for their contribution to this description.
anchor-contact: Questions? Get in touch with Gerard
